content.full_text:
April 17, 1981


Robert E. Roney
City Attorney
P.O. Box 1225
Portola, CA  96122

Dear Mr. Roney:

You have asked whether there is any potential for con-
flict under the Political Reform Act where the wife of a
City Council member is appointed to the City Planning Commis-
sion. Salary, reimbursement or per diem from a governmental
agency does not constitute income for purposes of the Political
Reform Act. Government Code Section 82030(b)(2). Therefore,
there is no need under Sections 87100 and 87103 for the City
Council member to disqualify himself on matters involving
the Planning Commission, or for his wife to disqualify her-
self on matters involving the City Council, merely because
of their official positions.

If you have any further questions, please feel free to
call me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,


Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:kp

City of Portola
P.O. BOX 1225
PORTOLA, CALIFORNIA 96122
Telephone: (916) 832-4217

April 15, 1981

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca.
95804

Attn: Barbara Milman,
Legal Division

Gentlemen:

The undersigned, as City Attorney for the City of Portola,
requests letter advice as to any possible disabling conflict
posed by the following factual context:

The wife of a member of the City Planning Commission was
appointed to the City Council. The Planning Commission members
are unpaid; the Council members receive nominal pay of fifty
dollars per month. Portola is a general law City with specific
local law applications to this situation.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Roney
City Attorney
(916) 832-5845

RER:jee
